name: ML Lifecycle: Problem to Production
summary: The end-to-end process for building an ML system — from defining the problem to monitoring the model in production. Every ML project follows this lifecycle, whether you are building a simple classifier or a complex LLM application.
whenToUseIt: Use this framework at the start of every ML project to ensure you have a clear plan from problem definition through to production monitoring. Reference it when you feel lost or stuck — it tells you what stage you are at and what comes next.
steps: Problem Definition — What are you trying to predict or generate? What business problem does it solve? What does success look like? Define metrics before writing any code.
Data Collection — Identify and gather the data you need. Is it available? Is it labelled? Is there enough of it? This step often takes longer than expected.
Data Preparation — Clean, transform, and engineer features. Handle missing values, encode categories, normalise numbers. Split into train/validation/test sets.
Model Development — Choose an algorithm, train the model, tune hyperparameters. Start simple (baseline model) and iterate toward complexity.
Evaluation — Test the model on held-out data. Check metrics (accuracy, F1, etc.), look for bias, test edge cases. Compare against the baseline.
Deployment — Serve the model via an API, batch pipeline, or edge device. Containerise, set up CI/CD, implement health checks.
Monitoring — Track model performance over time. Watch for data drift, performance degradation, and unexpected inputs. Set up alerts.
example: Building a product recommendation system for an Australian e-commerce retailer: (1) Problem: increase average order value by suggesting relevant products — success metric is click-through rate on recommendations. (2) Data: 2 years of purchase history, product catalogue, user browsing data. (3) Preparation: create user-product interaction matrix, encode product categories, handle cold-start users. (4) Model: start with collaborative filtering baseline, iterate to a neural recommendation model. (5) Evaluation: A/B test against the existing 'popular products' section — did CTR increase? (6) Deploy: real-time API that returns top-5 recommendations per user. (7) Monitor: track CTR daily, retrain monthly as new purchase data arrives.
